functions.name: nngeometry.generator.jacobian.grads_conv.conv_backward
documentation:
Computes per-example gradients for nn.Conv1d and nn.Conv2d layers.

This function is used in the internal behaviour of bnn.Linear.

functions.name: nngeometry.utils.grad
documentation:
Computes the gradient of `output` with respect to the `PVector` `vec`

..warning This function only works when internally your `vec` has been

: created from leaf nodes in the graph (e.g. model parameters)

```
param output

The scalar quantity to be differentiated

param vec

a 

return

a  of gradients of  w.r.t 
```

classes.name: nngeometry.layers.Cosine1d
documentation:
Computes the cosine similarity between rows of the weight matrix
and the incoming data

classes.name: nngeometry.layers.WeightNorm1d
documentation:
Computes an affine mapping of the incoming data using a weight matrix
with rows normalized with norm 1

classes.name: nngeometry.layers.WeightNorm2d
documentation:
Computes a 2d convolution using a kernel weight matrix
with rows normalized with norm 1

classes.name: nngeometry.layers.Affine1d
documentation:
Computes the transformation out = weight * input + bias
where * is the elementwise multiplication. This is similar to the
scaling and translation given by parameters gamma and beta in batch norm

classes.name: nngeometry.generator.jacobian.Jacobian
documentation:
Computes jacobians $\mathbf{J}_{ijk}=\frac{\partial f\left(x_{j}\right)_{i}}{\delta\mathbf{w}_{k}}$,
FIM matrices $\mathbf{F}_{k,k'}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i,j}\mathbf{J}_{ijk}\mathbf{J}_{ijk'}$
and NTK matrices $\mathbf{K}_{iji'j'}=\sum_{k}\mathbf{J}_{ijk}\mathbf{J}_{ijk'}$.

This generator is written in pure PyTorch and exploits some tricks in order to make computations
more efficient.

```
param layer_collection



type layer_collection



param model



type model

Pytorch 

param function

A function  where  are minibatchs
returned by the dataloader (Note that in some cases  are not required). If None,
it defaults to 

type function

python function

param n_output

How many output is there for each example of your function. E.g. in 10 class
classification this would probably be 10.

type n_output

integer
```

functions.name: nngeometry.metrics.FIM_MonteCarlo
documentation:
Helper that creates a matrix computing the Fisher Information
Matrix using a Monte-Carlo estimate of y|x with `trials` samples per
example

Parameters
----------
model : torch.nn.Module
    The model that contains all parameters of the function
loader : torch.utils.data.DataLoader
    DataLoader for computing expectation over the input space
representation : class
    The parameter matrix representation that will be used to store
    the matrix
variants : string 'classif_logits' or 'regression', optional
        (default='classif_logits')
    Variant to use depending on how you interpret your function.
    Possible choices are:
     - 'classif_logits' when using logits for classification
     - 'classif_logsoftmax' when using log_softmax values for classification
     - 'segmentation_logits' when using logits in a segmentation task
trials : int, optional (default=1)
    Number of trials for Monte Carlo sampling
device : string, optional (default='cpu')
    Target device for the returned matrix
function : function, optional (default=None)
    An optional function if different from `model(input)`. If
    it is different from None, it will override the device
    parameter.
layer_collection : layercollection.LayerCollection, optional
        (default=None)
    An optional layer collection

functions.name: nngeometry.metrics.FIM
documentation:
Helper that creates a matrix computing the Fisher Information
Matrix using closed form expressions for the expectation y|x
as described in (Pascanu and Bengio, 2013)

Parameters
----------
model : torch.nn.Module
    The model that contains all parameters of the function
loader : torch.utils.data.DataLoader
    DataLoader for computing expectation over the input space
representation : class
    The parameter matrix representation that will be used to store
    the matrix
n_output : int
    Number of outputs of the model
variants : string 'classif_logits' or 'regression', optional
        (default='classif_logits')
    Variant to use depending on how you interpret your function.
    Possible choices are:
     - 'classif_logits' when using logits for classification
     - 'regression' when using a gaussian regression model
device : string, optional (default='cpu')
    Target device for the returned matrix
function : function, optional (default=None)
    An optional function if different from `model(input)`. If
    it is different from None, it will override the device
    parameter.
layer_collection : layercollection.LayerCollection, optional
        (default=None)
    An optional layer collection

functions.name: nngeometry.object.vector.random_pvector_dict
documentation:
Returns a random {class}`nngeometry.object.PVector` object using
the structure defined by the `layer_collection` parameter, with
each components drawn from a normal distribution with mean 0 and standard
deviation 1.

The returned `PVector` will internally use a dict representation.

```
param layer_collection

The 
```

describing the structure of the random pvector

methods.name: nngeometry.object.vector.PVector.get_flat_representation
documentation:
Returns a Pytorch 1d tensor of the flatten vector.

:::{warning}
The ordering in which the parameters are
flattened can seem to be arbitrary. It is in fact
the same ordering as specified by the `layercollection.LayerCollection`
object.
:::

```
return
```

```pycon
>>> M.size()
(1254, 1254)
>>> M.size(0)
1254
```

methods.name: nngeometry.object.pspace.PMatAbstract._check_data_examples
documentation:
Either data or examples has to be not None in order
to populate the matrix. If both are not None, then
it is ambiguous, then the following test will fail

A $d \times d$ matrix in parameter space. This abstract class
defines common methods used in concrete representations.

```
param generator

The generator

type generator



param data

if None, it requires examples to be different from None, and
it uses the generator to populate the matrix data

param examples

if data is None, it uses these examples to populate the
matrix using the generator.  is either a Dataloader, or a
single mini-batch of (inputs, targets) from a Dataloader
```

Note:

: Either `data` or `examples` has to be different from None, and both
  cannot be not None at the same time.

methods.name: nngeometry.object.pspace.PMatKFAC.get_dense_tensor
documentation:
- split_weight_bias (bool): if True then the parameters are ordered in
the same way as in the dense or blockdiag representation, but it
involves more operations. Otherwise the coefficients corresponding
to the bias are mixed between coefficients of the weight matrix

the same way as in the dense or blockdiag representation, but it

to the bias are mixed between coefficients of the weight matrix

methods.name: nngeometry.object.pspace.PMatEKFAC.get_KFE
documentation:
Returns a dict index by layers, of dense eigenvectors constructed from
Kronecker-factored eigenvectors

- split_weight_bias (bool): if True then the parameters are ordered in
the same way as in the dense or blockdiag representation, but it
involves more operations. Otherwise the coefficients corresponding
to the bias are mixed between coefficients of the weight matrix

methods.name: nngeometry.object.pspace.PMatEKFAC.update_diag
documentation:
Will update the diagonal in the KFE (aka the approximate eigenvalues)
using current values of the model's parameters

EKFAC representation from
*George, Laurent et al., Fast Approximate Natural Gradient Descent
in a Kronecker-factored Eigenbasis, NIPS 2018*

classes.name: nngeometry.object.pspace.PMatImplicit
documentation:
PMatImplicit is a very special representation, since
no elements of the matrix is ever computed, but instead
various linear algebra operations are performed implicitely
using efficient tricks.

The computations are done exactly, meaning that there is
no approximation involved. This is useful for networks too big
to fit in memory.

classes.name: nngeometry.object.pspace.PMatQuasiDiag
documentation:
Quasidiagonal approximation as decribed in Ollivier,
Riemannian metrics for neural networks I: feedforward networks,
Information and Inference: A Journal of the IMA, 2015
